constraints: The adjacency matrix is symmetric and has '0' on the diagonal.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains an integer N (1 ≤ N ≤ 2000) – the number of users. Then follow N lines, each line is a string of N characters consisting of '0' or '1'. The j‑th character of the i‑th line is '1' if user i and user j are already friends, otherwise '0'. The matrix is symmetric and diagonal entries are '0'.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of friendship suggestions Ilya has to send.

sampleTestCases.explanation: User 1 is already friends with everyone, so no suggestions from him. Users 2,3,4 each have two non‑friend pairs that share user 1 as a common friend, giving 3 unordered suggestions.
All three users are already friends with each other; there is no pair without a direct friendship, hence there is no suggestion.
The network forms a chain 1‑2‑3‑4‑5. The non‑friend pairs (1,3), (2,4) and (3,5) each have a common neighbour, resulting in three suggestions.

solution.approach: A suggestion is counted for an unordered pair (u,v) that are not friends yet and share at least one common friend w (w ≠ u, v). For each user we store the set of his friends as a bitset of length N. For every unordered pair (u,v) with matrix[u][v]=='0' we intersect the two bitsets; if the intersection is non‑empty the pair contributes one suggestion. Using C++ std::bitset (or an array of 64‑bit blocks in C) the intersection test is O(N/wordSize). Overall complexity per test case is O(N²·(N/wordSize)) = O(N³/64), which fits the limits for N=2000.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N³/64) per test case (≈1.25·10⁸ elementary operations for N=2000).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N²/64) for storing the bitsets (≈0.5 MB for N=2000).
